# January-July 2026 almanac briefing for a newsroom

The first half of 2026 delivered a dense run of headline events across sports, entertainment and space: the biggest men’s World Cup ever is now in its knockout phase across North America; the Seahawks won a defense-heavy Super Bowl LX; the Knicks ended a 53-year title drought in the NBA Finals; the Oscars split their biggest honors between *One Battle After Another* and *Sinners*; and NASA’s Artemis II sent humans farther from Earth than any mission since Apollo, then brought them home safely. What follows is a single briefing built for editors and producers, with dates, scores, names and records grounded in published sources.

---

## 1) The 2026 FIFA World Cup so far

### The format, hosts and scale
The 2026 men’s World Cup is the first with **48 teams**, up from 32, and the first to use **12 groups of four** followed by a new **Round of 32**, then the Round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals and final. The top two teams in each group plus the **eight best third-place teams** advance to the Round of 32. The tournament runs from **11 June to 19 July 2026** and is jointly hosted by **Canada, Mexico and the United States**. FIFA says the expanded format produces **104 matches**, and the champion must now win **eight matches** rather than seven. Sources: FIFA format explainer https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/article-fifa-world-cup-2026-mexico-canada-usa-new-format-tournament-football-soccer ; FIFA tournament guide https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/fifa-world-cup-2026-hosts-cities-dates-usa-mexico-canada

FIFA lists **16 host cities**: **Toronto** and **Vancouver** in Canada; **Guadalajara, Mexico City and Monterrey** in Mexico; and **Atlanta, Boston, Dallas, Houston, Kansas City, Los Angeles, Miami, New York/New Jersey, Philadelphia, San Francisco Bay Area and Seattle** in the U.S. Source: FIFA host-city guide https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/article-fifa-world-cup-2026-mexico-canada-usa-new-format-tournament-football-soccer

### The knockout rounds so far: results, scares and upsets

The new Round of 32 has already produced several defining storylines.

- **Canada 1-0 South Africa (June 28)**: Canada reached the last 16 on a **92nd-minute Stephen Eustaquio goal**, which Reuters said sent the hosts into the World Cup last 16 for the first time. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/canada-score-late-winner-advance-last-16-world-cup-2026-06-28

- **Morocco 1-1 Netherlands, Morocco won 3-2 on penalties (June 29)**: Morocco eliminated the Dutch in Monterrey. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/morocco-advance-world-cup-after-penalty-shootout-win-over-netherlands-2026-06-30

- **Germany 1-1 Paraguay, Paraguay won 4-3 on penalties (June 29)**: one of the tournament’s clearest shocks. Reuters described Germany’s exit as a **major upset** and noted it was the **first time Germany had lost a World Cup penalty shootout**. Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/paraguay-knock-germany-out-world-cup-penalties-major-upset-2026-06-29 and https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/paraguay-pull-off-world-cup-penalty-gamble-last-historic-germany-win-2026-06-30

- **Mexico 2-0 Ecuador (June 30)**: goals from **Julian Quiñones** and **Raúl Jiménez** gave co-host Mexico its first World Cup knockout win in **40 years**, according to Reuters. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/mexico-beat-ecuador-2-0-reach-world-cup-last-16-end-knockout-hoodoo-2026-07-01

- **France 3-0 Sweden (June 30)**: Reuters reported a **Kylian Mbappé double** as France eased through. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/mbappe-double-france-ease-past-sweden-3-0-reach-last-16-2026-06-30

- **Norway 2-1 Ivory Coast (June 30)**: **Erling Haaland** scored the **86th-minute winner**, delivering Norway’s **first World Cup knockout-round victory**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/haaland-hero-again-norway-edge-ivory-coast-advance-world-cup-2026-06-30

- **Belgium 3-2 Senegal after extra time (July 1)**: Belgium trailed **2-0** and survived only through a **125th-minute Youri Tielemans penalty**, one of the wildest escapes of the tournament so far. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/belgium-through-world-cup-last-16-with-dramatic-3-2-win-over-senegal-2026-07-01

- **United States 2-0 Bosnia and Herzegovina (July 1)**: Reuters said the **10-man U.S.** ended a **24-year wait for a knockout-stage World Cup win**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/update1-soccer-us-keep-world-cup-dream-alive-with-gutsy-win-over-bosnia-2026-07-02

- **England 2-1 DR Congo (July 1)**: **Harry Kane** scored twice in the final 15 minutes after DR Congo had threatened “one of the tournament’s biggest upsets,” Reuters wrote. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/kane-double-rescues-england-comeback-win-over-dr-congo-2026-07-01

- **Spain 3-0 Austria (July 2)**: Spain advanced comfortably. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/spain-sweep-aside-austria-3-0-reach-world-cup-last-16-2026-07-02

- **Portugal 2-1 Croatia (July 2)**: Reuters reported **Cristiano Ronaldo** equalized from the penalty spot before **Gonçalo Ramos** headed a stoppage-time winner. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/portugals-martinez-takes-off-ronaldo-when-push-comes-shove-2026-07-03 ; FIFA match report https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/portugal-croatia-match-report-highlights

- **Switzerland 2-0 Algeria (July 3)**: goals from **Breel Embolo** and **Dan Ndoye**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/slick-switzerland-sweep-past-algeria-into-world-cup-last-16-2026-07-03

- **Egypt 1-1 Australia, Egypt won 4-2 on penalties (July 3)**: Reuters said it was **Egypt’s first World Cup knockout win**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/egypt-beat-australia-penalties-reach-world-cup-round-16-2026-07-03

- **Argentina 3-2 Cabo Verde after extra time (July 3)**: Reuters reported the defending champions needed an **extra-time own goal** to survive a major scare against World Cup debutants Cabo Verde. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/argentina-beat-cape-verde-3-2-extra-time-thriller-2026-07-04

- **Colombia 1-0 Ghana (July 3)**: **Jhon Arias** scored the winner to secure the final last-16 spot. Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/colombia-beat-ghana-1-0-set-up-switzerland-showdown-2026-07-04 ; FIFA roundup https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/matchday-23-round-up-review-highlights

### The upcoming Round-of-16 schedule
FIFA’s knockout calendar places the Round of 16 across **4-7 July 2026**. As of July 4, the confirmed slate is:

- **Saturday, July 4** — **Canada vs Morocco**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/captain-davies-bench-canada-last-16-clash-with-morocco-2026-07-04 ; FIFA live updates hub https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/todays-matches-live-updates

- **Saturday, July 4** — **Paraguay vs France**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/deschamps-warns-extreme-heat-could-test-france-against-paraguay-in-last-16-2026-07-03 ; FIFA knockout bracket https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket

- **Sunday, July 5** — **Brazil vs Norway**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/brazil-face-haaland-norway-hoodoo-world-cup-last-16-2026-07-03 ; Reuters schedule graphic https://www.reuters.com/graphics/SOCCER-WORLDCUP/mopaoglqkpa/schedule

- **Sunday, July 5** — **Mexico vs England**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/mexico-chase-another-slice-world-cup-history-england-return-azteca-2026-07-03 ; https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/sundays-mexico-england-game-be-moved-earlier-due-storm-risk-local-media-report-2026-07-03/

- **Monday, July 6** — **United States vs Belgium**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/belgium-must-improve-against-confident-co-hosts-us-after-senegal-late-show-2026-07-02 ; https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/belgium-wary-what-comes-next-against-us-after-senegal-let-off-2026-07-03

- **Monday, July 6** — **Spain vs Portugal**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/spain-sweep-aside-austria-3-0-reach-world-cup-last-16-2026-07-02 ; https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/portugal-relieved-unconvincing-spain-loom-world-cup-last-16-2026-07-03

- **Tuesday, July 7** — **Argentina vs Egypt**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/argentina-beat-cape-verde-3-2-extra-time-thriller-2026-07-04 ; Reuters schedule graphic https://www.reuters.com/graphics/SOCCER-WORLDCUP/mopaoglqkpa/schedule

- **Tuesday, July 7** — **Switzerland vs Colombia**  
  Sources: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/sports/soccer/colombia-beat-ghana-1-0-set-up-switzerland-showdown-2026-07-04 ; Reuters schedule graphic https://www.reuters.com/graphics/SOCCER-WORLDCUP/mopaoglqkpa/schedule

The biggest editorial takeaways so far: the expansion has created more knockout drama without eliminating heavyweight survival; **Germany are out**, **Mexico broke its knockout curse**, **Egypt made national history**, and **Argentina, Belgium and England all had to escape serious danger**.

---

## 2) Super Bowl LX

**Super Bowl LX** was played on **Sunday, February 8, 2026**, at **Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara, California**. The **Seattle Seahawks** beat the **New England Patriots**, **29-13**. Sources: NFL Super Bowl page https://www.nfl.com/super-bowl ; NFL photos page confirming venue/date https://www.nfl.com/photos/best-of-super-bowl-lx

The game’s **MVP** was Seahawks running back **Kenneth Walker III**, who the NFL says rushed for **135 yards on 27 carries** and added **26 receiving yards** for **161 scrimmage yards**. NFL.com notes he became the **first running back to win Super Bowl MVP since Terrell Davis in Super Bowl XXXII**, and that his **135 rushing yards were the most in a Super Bowl since Davis ran for 157**. Source: NFL.com https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp

NFL.com also highlighted a scoring oddity central to the Seahawks’ win: kicker **Jason Myers** made a **Super Bowl-record five field goals**. Source: NFL Super Bowl hub https://www.nfl.com/super-bowl

---

## 3) The 2026 NBA Finals

The **New York Knicks** beat the **San Antonio Spurs**, **4-1**, in the 2026 NBA Finals. The series began on **June 3, 2026**, and ended in **Game 5 on June 13**, when New York won **94-90 in San Antonio**. Sources: NBA Finals schedule page https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-schedule ; Game 5 summary https://www.nba.com/game/nyk-vs-sas-0042500405 ; NBA Finals latest page https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest

The clinching game belonged to **Jalen Brunson**, who scored a **Knicks Finals-record 45 points** in Game 5 and was named **2026 NBA Finals MVP**, winning the **Bill Russell Trophy**. Sources: NBA Finals MVP story https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp ; NBA latest page https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest

The historical significance is unusually large:
- It was the **Knicks’ first NBA title since 1973**, ending a **53-year championship drought**. Sources: NBA.com https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-schedule ; https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest
- It was the **franchise’s third NBA championship**. Source: NBA history framing on NBA.com latest coverage https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/latest
- Game 4 became a signature moment: New York came back from **29 points down** to beat San Antonio **107-106** on **OG Anunoby’s tip-in with 1.2 seconds left**, which AP called a **record-breaking comeback in an NBA Finals game**. Source: NBA/AP game recap https://www.nba.com/game/sas-vs-nyk-0042500404

Series scores:
- **Game 1:** Knicks 105, Spurs 95  
- **Game 2:** Knicks 105, Spurs 104  
- **Game 3:** Spurs 115, Knicks 111  
- **Game 4:** Knicks 107, Spurs 106  
- **Game 5:** Knicks 94, Spurs 90  
Source: NBA Finals game summaries https://www.nba.com/game/sas-vs-nyk-0042500404 and https://www.nba.com/news/2026-nba-finals-schedule

---

## 4) The 98th Academy Awards

The **98th Academy Awards** were held on **Sunday, March 15, 2026**. The ceremony was hosted by **Conan O’Brien**. Sources: Academy key dates https://www.oscars.org/oscars/98th-oscars-and-academy-key-dates ; PBS/AP winners list https://www.pbs.org/newshour/arts/heres-a-full-list-of-2026-academy-awards-winners

The night’s dominant films were clearly **Paul Thomas Anderson’s *One Battle After Another*** and **Ryan Coogler’s *Sinners***. Reuters reported that *One Battle After Another* won **six Oscars**, *Sinners* won **four**, and *Frankenstein* won **three**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16/

### Major-category winners
- **Best Picture:** *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Director:** **Paul Thomas Anderson**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Actor:** **Michael B. Jordan**, *Sinners*  
- **Best Actress:** **Jessie Buckley**, *Hamnet*  
- **Best Supporting Actor:** **Sean Penn**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Supporting Actress:** **Amy Madigan**, *Weapons*  
- **Best Original Screenplay:** **Ryan Coogler**, *Sinners*  
- **Best Adapted Screenplay:** **Paul Thomas Anderson**, *One Battle After Another*  
Sources: Reuters winners list https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16/ ; PBS/AP list https://www.pbs.org/newshour/arts/heres-a-full-list-of-2026-academy-awards-winners

### Other major wins and films that dominated
- *One Battle After Another* also won **Best Casting** and **Best Film Editing**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16/
- *Sinners* also won **Best Original Score** for **Ludwig Göransson** and **Best Cinematography** for **Autumn Durald Arkapaw**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16/
- *Frankenstein* won **Production Design**, **Makeup and Hairstyling**, and **Costume Design**. Source: Reuters https://www.reuters.com/lifestyle/oscars-2026-winners-list-sinners-one-battle-after-another-sweep-2026-03-16/
- **Best Animated Feature:** *KPop Demon Hunters*  
- **Best International Feature:** *Sentimental Value* (Norway)  
- **Best Documentary Feature:** *Mr. Nobody Against Putin*  
Source: PBS/AP list https://www.pbs.org/newshour/arts/heres-a-full-list-of-2026-academy-awards-winners

One notable context line for entertainment desks: PBS/AP reported that *Sinners* entered the night with a **record-breaking 16 nominations**. Source: https://www.pbs.org/newshour/arts/heres-a-full-list-of-2026-academy-awards-winners

---

## 5) NASA’s Artemis II

NASA’s **Artemis II** launched on **April 1, 2026, at 6:35 p.m. EDT** from **Launch Pad 39B at Kennedy Space Center** aboard the **Space Launch System**. NASA describes it as the **first crewed Artemis mission** and the first human mission toward the Moon since the Apollo era. Sources: NASA release on farthest-distance record https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight ; NASA Flight Day 10 blog https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown/

### Crew
The four-person crew was:
- **Reid Wiseman** (NASA)
- **Victor Glover** (NASA)
- **Christina Koch** (NASA)
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency)

Source: NASA release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight

### Record and mission profile
On **April 6, 2026**, six days into the mission, Artemis II passed **248,655 miles from Earth**, surpassing the record set by **Apollo 13** in 1970. NASA said Orion would reach a farthest point of about **252,756 miles** from Earth, setting a new record for human spaceflight distance. Source: NASA release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight

NASA also said the crew would pass within about **4,067 miles** of the Moon’s surface at closest approach and see parts of the lunar far side “with human eyes” for the first time in the Artemis era. Source: NASA release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight

### Outcome
Artemis II ended with a planned **splashdown off the coast of San Diego on April 10, 2026, at 8:07 p.m. EDT (5:07 p.m. PDT)**. NASA’s Flight Day 10 blog said Orion completed a **694,481-mile journey** before recovery by NASA and U.S. military teams, who transferred the astronauts by helicopter to the **USS John P. Murtha** for post-mission medical evaluations. Source: NASA blog https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown/

The cleanest summary for space desks: Artemis II was a **successful crewed lunar test flight** that **broke the human-distance record**, validated Orion in deep space, looped four astronauts around the Moon, and **returned them safely to Earth**. Sources: NASA release https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-crew-eclipses-record-for-farthest-human-spaceflight ; NASA blog https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/10/artemis-ii-flight-day-10-crew-sets-for-final-burn-splashdown/

---

## Bottom line

Across January-July 2026, the defining cross-desk themes were scale, drought-breaking and return: the **48-team World Cup** delivered immediate knockout volatility, the **Seahawks** won Super Bowl LX **29-13**, the **Knicks** won their first title since **1973**, the Oscars were dominated by **One Battle After Another** and **Sinners**, and **Artemis II** carried humans farther from Earth than any mission since Apollo and brought them home safely.

Short direct answer: the first half of 2026 was defined by an expanded World Cup full of upsets and escapes, Seattle’s Super Bowl win, New York’s long-awaited NBA title, an Oscars split between *One Battle After Another* and *Sinners*, and NASA’s successful record-setting Artemis II lunar mission.

This is a single, wide-coverage briefing on the major sports, entertainment and space storylines that defined the first half of 2026, with emphasis on the specific events requested.

---

## 1) 2026 FIFA World Cup so far

### The big frame: biggest World Cup ever, across three host countries
The 2026 FIFA World Cup is the first men’s World Cup with **48 teams**, up from 32, and the first to be **co-hosted by three countries: Canada, Mexico and the United States**. FIFA says the tournament runs from **11 June to 19 July 2026**, includes **104 matches**, and uses a new structure: **12 groups of four**, followed by a **Round of 32**, then the Round of 16, quarter-finals, semi-finals and final. FIFA also notes that **Mexico City Stadium** hosts the opener and **New York New Jersey Stadium** the final.  
Sources: FIFA on hosts/dates/cities: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/fifa-world-cup-2026-hosts-cities-dates-usa-mexico-canada ; FIFA on the 48-team format: https://www.fifa.com/en/articles/article-fifa-world-cup-2026-mexico-canada-usa-new-format-tournament-football-soccer ; FIFA match schedule: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/match-schedule-fixtures-results-teams-stadiums

### How the knockout stage has gone
As of **4 July 2026**, the **Round of 32 has been completed** and the **Round of 16 is set**. FIFA’s knockout bracket and match round-ups show a tournament already defined by a few clear themes: heavyweights surviving, at least one major shootout upset, and several tight eliminators.  
Sources: FIFA knockout bracket: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/knockout-stage-match-schedule-bracket ; FIFA matchday round-ups: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/matchday-20-round-up-review-highlights ; https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/articles/matchday-23-round-up-review-highlights

### Specific Round-of-32 results and notable upsets
Confirmed Round-of-32 results from FIFA reporting and bracket listings include:

- **Canada 1-0 South Africa** — co-host Canada advanced, with FIFA framing it as a home-nation push into the last 16.  
  Source: FIFA home page/bracket summary: https://www.fifa.com/en/home

- **Brazil 2-1 Japan** — Brazil advanced in a closer-than-expected match.  
  Source: FIFA home page/bracket summary: https://www.fifa.com/en/home

- **Paraguay def. Germany on penalties, 4-3 after shootout** — one of the clearest shocks of the knockout stage so far. FIFA’s coverage specifically highlighted **Orlando Gill** celebrating after **Jonathan Tah** missed Germany’s sixth penalty in Foxborough.  
  Sources: FIFA World Cup page/spotlight: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup ; FIFA highlights hub: https://www.fifa.com/en/tournaments/mens/worldcup/canadamexicousa2026/highlights

- **Morocco def. Netherlands on penalties, 3-2 after shootout** — another major elimination of a European power.

## 2) Super Bowl LX

**Super Bowl LX** was played on **8 February 2026** at **Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara, California**. The **Seattle Seahawks** beat the **New England Patriots, 29-13**. **Kenneth Walker III** was named **Super Bowl MVP** after rushing for **135 yards on 27 carries** and finishing with **161 scrimmage yards**. NFL.com says Walker’s 135 rushing yards were the most in a Super Bowl since Terrell Davis rushed for 157 nearly three decades earlier.  
Sources: NFL MVP story: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-rb-kenneth-walker-iii-named-super-bowl-lx-mvp ; NFL photo package confirming date/venue/opponents: https://www.nfl.com/photos/best-of-super-bowl-lx ; Pro Football Reference box score: https://www.pro-football-reference.com/boxscores/202602080nwe.htm

The game was defined less by quarterback play than by **Seattle’s defense** and rushing control. NFL coverage described the Seahawks as taking a **19-0 lead**, recording **six sacks** and **three takeaways**, and largely neutralizing Patriots quarterback **Drake Maye**.  
Source: NFL recap/what we learned: https://www.nfl.com/news/seahawks-patriots-in-super-bowl-lx-what-we-learned-from-seattle-s-29-13-win

**Newsroom angle:** Seattle’s second Lombardi came on a throwback formula — defense, field position and a power running game — rather than a shootout.

---

## 3) The 2026 NBA Finals

The **New York Knicks** beat the **San Antonio Spurs** in **five games, 4-1**, to win the **2026 NBA championship**. The clincher was **Game 5 on 13 June 2026**, when the Knicks won **94-90** in San Antonio.  
Sources: NBA Finals hub: https://www.nba.com/playoffs/2026/nba-finals ; official Game 5 page: https://www.nba.com/game/nyk-vs-sas-0042500405 ; Basketball-Reference series summary: https://www.basketball-reference.com/playoffs/2026-nba-finals-knicks-vs-spurs.html

### Clinching game
In the clincher, **Jalen Brunson scored 45 points**, including a dominant fourth quarter, and was named **Finals MVP**. NBA.com says Brunson’s 45 points set a **Knicks Finals record** for a title-clinching performance and secured the **Bill Russell Trophy**.  
Sources: Finals MVP story: https://www.nba.com/news/jalen-brunson-wins-bill-russell-trophy-as-2026-nba-finals-mvp ; Game 5 live recap: https://www.nba.com/news/live-updates-2026-nba-finals-game-5-knicks-seek-first-title-since-1973

### Historical significance
This title mattered well beyond the box score:

- It was the **Knicks’ first championship since 1973**, ending a **53-year drought**.  
- NBA.com described the Knicks as becoming champions “for the first time in 53 years.”  
- League game notes say New York became the **first team since 1971 to win the NBA Finals after trailing by 10 or more points in every game of the series**.  
- NBA.com also highlighted the Knicks’ **largest comeback in NBA Finals history** during the series and a record **ninth consecutive road playoff win** in a single postseason.  
Sources: NBA recap and takeaways: https://www.nba.com/news/4-takeaways-knicks-spurs-game-5 ; NBA Finals game notes: https://pr.nba.com/2026-nba-finals-game-notes ; NBA Finals recap collection: https://www.nba.com/watch/list/collection/2026-nba-finals-game-recap-videos

**Newsroom angle:** in a New York sports year already crowded with big narratives, the Knicks’ title was probably the most emotionally resonant — a historic franchise finally ending one of the league’s longest active championship droughts.

---

## 4) The 98th Academy Awards

The **98th Academy Awards** were held on **Sunday, 15 March 2026**, at the **Dolby Theatre at Ovation Hollywood** in Los Angeles. The ceremony was **hosted by Conan O’Brien**, who returned after hosting the previous year.  
Sources: Academy press release on host/airdate: http://press.oscars.org/news/host-conan-obrien-and-executive-producers-raj-kapoor-and-katy-mullan-reunite-98th-oscarsr ; Academy press release on production and date: http://press.oscars.org/news/98th-oscarsr-production-team-announced ; Academy ceremony page: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026

### The film that dominated
The night belonged to **“One Battle After Another,”** which won **Best Picture** and finished with **six Oscars**, including **Best Director** for **Paul Thomas Anderson**, **Best Adapted Screenplay**, **Best Supporting Actor** for **Sean Penn**, and the inaugural **Best Casting** award.  
Sources: Academy ceremony page: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026 ; ABC News winners summary: https://abcnews.com/GMA/Culture/oscars-2026-full-winners-list/story?id=130769299 ; ABC7 winners summary: https://abc7.com/post/oscars-2026-winners-list-see-won-best-picture-director-actor-actress-more/18711211

### Major-category winners
Across the headline categories, the major winners were:

- **Best Picture:** *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Director:** **Paul Thomas Anderson**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Actor:** **Michael B. Jordan**, *Sinners*  
- **Best Actress:** **Jessie Buckley**, *Hamnet*  
- **Best Supporting Actor:** **Sean Penn**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Supporting Actress:** **Amy Madigan**, *Weapons*  
Sources: Academy ceremony page: https://www.oscars.org/oscars/ceremonies/2026 ; ABC News full winners list: https://abcnews.com/GMA/Culture/oscars-2026-full-winners-list/story?id=130769299 ; Los Angeles Times live winners coverage: https://www.latimes.com/entertainment-arts/awards/live/oscars-2026-live-updates-winners-list

### Other notable winners and storylines
Two additional storylines stood out:

1. **“Sinners”** came in as a giant contender and left with **four Oscars**, including **Original Screenplay** for **Ryan Coogler** and a historic **Cinematography** win for **Autumn Durald Arkapaw**, described in coverage as the **first woman to win that Oscar**.  
   Sources: ABC News summary: https://abcnews.com/GMA/Culture/oscars-2026-full-winners-list/story?id=130769299 ; ABC7 recap: https://abc7news.com/post/oscars-2026-battle-another-wins-best-picture-98th-academy-awards/18719481

2. **“KPop Demon Hunters”** won **Best Animated Feature**, and its song **“Golden”** won **Best Original Song**, widely noted as the **first K-pop song** to win in that category.  
   Sources: ABC.com winners list: https://abc.com/news/e964adff-12f7-4a53-b828-3ec9db4be197/category/3590739 ; Hollywood Reporter recap video summary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=XK9LGNjPntM&vl=en

**Newsroom angle:** the Oscars crystallized a year in which prestige auteurs, music-driven pop cinema, and Black-led studio filmmaking all landed real awards traction — but the final coronation went to Paul Thomas Anderson.

---

## 5) NASA’s Artemis II

**Artemis II** was NASA’s first **crewed** Artemis mission and the first human mission to travel to the Moon and back since **Apollo 17 in 1972**. NASA’s published launch-window material shows the mission launched on **1 April 2026**, and NASA announced the crew **splashed down on 10 April 2026** in the Pacific after a roughly **10-day mission**.  
Sources: NASA mission availability schedule: https://www.nasa.gov/wp-content/uploads/2026/01/artemis-ii-mission-availability.pdf ; NASA mission coverage page: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/artemis/artemis-2/nasa-sets-coverage-for-artemis-ii-moon-mission ; NASA splashdown release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth

### Crew
The four-person crew was:

- **Reid Wiseman** — commander  
- **Victor Glover** — pilot  
- **Christina Koch** — mission specialist  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency) — mission specialist  
Sources: NASA splashdown release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth ; NASA gallery: https://www.nasa.gov/gallery/artemis-ii-splashdown-and-recovery

### Records and milestones
NASA said Artemis II **surpassed the Apollo 13 record for the farthest crewed spaceflight**, making Wiseman, Glover, Koch and Hansen the humans who traveled **farther from Earth than any people in history**. NASA also described the crew as capturing **never-before-seen views of the far side of the Moon**.  
Sources: NASA splashdown release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth ; NASA post-splashdown news conference description: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ir2X1-FjDxg

### Outcome
The mission was a **success**. NASA said all four astronauts returned in **good health** after a **record-setting moon flyby**, and the mission demonstrated Orion’s human-rated systems ahead of future lunar-landing Artemis missions. NASA’s flight-day updates show the crew swung around the Moon on **6 April 2026**, exited the lunar sphere of influence on **7 April**, and returned to Earth on **10 April**.  
Sources: NASA splashdown release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth ; NASA flight day 9 update: https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/09/artemis-ii-flight-day-9-crew-prepares-to-come-home

**Newsroom angle:** Artemis II was the year’s clearest space milestone — a successful crewed return to deep-space lunar flight, and the strongest sign yet that the post-Apollo moon program is now operational rather than aspirational.

---

## Bottom line for editors

Across sports, entertainment and space, the first half of 2026 has been defined by **scale, revival and return**: the biggest World Cup ever, the Knicks ending a 53-year title drought, the Seahawks winning behind old-school defense and rushing, the Oscars crowning a long-admired auteur, and NASA sending humans around the Moon again for the first time in more than half a century.

Short answer: the defining Jan–July 2026 stories were the expanded World Cup’s upset-filled knockout stage, Seattle’s 29-13 Super Bowl win, the Knicks’ 4-1 Finals title over San Antonio, “One Battle After Another” dominating the March 15 Oscars, and Artemis II’s successful April 1–10 crewed moon flyby.

The **2026 NBA Finals** matched the **New York Knicks** against the **San Antonio Spurs**. New York won the series **4-1**, clinching the title in **Game 5 on 13 June 2026** with a **94-90 road win** in San Antonio.

The clinching game belonged to **Jalen Brunson**, who scored **45 points**. The NBA identified him as **Finals MVP**, and its stats page notes that his **45 points tied Michael Jordan’s 1998 mark for the most points on the road in an NBA Finals-clinching game**.

### Historical significance
This title was historically important for several reasons:

- It was the **Knicks’ first NBA championship since 1973**, ending a **53-year drought**.  
- It was the franchise’s **third championship**.  
- The Finals included one of the series’ signature moments in **Game 4**, when the Knicks came back from **29 points down** to win **107-106**, which NBA coverage described as the **largest comeback in NBA Finals history**.  
- NBA.com also called the Knicks the first team to win an NBA “**double**” — the **NBA Cup** and the **NBA title** in the same season.  

Sources:

## 4) The 98th Academy Awards

The **98th Academy Awards** were held on **15 March 2026** at the **Dolby Theatre in Hollywood, Los Angeles** and were hosted by **Conan O’Brien**, his second consecutive time as host.  
Sources:

### Major winners
Official and network winner lists show these headline results:

- **Best Picture**: **One Battle After Another**  
- **Best Director**: **Paul Thomas Anderson**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Actor**: **Sean Penn**, *One Battle After Another*  
- **Best Actress**: **Jessie Buckley**, *Hamnet*  
- **Best Supporting Actress**: **Amy Madigan**, *Weapons*  
- **Best Original Screenplay**: **Ryan Coogler**, *Sinners*  
- **Best Animated Feature**: **KPop Demon Hunters**  
- **Best Original Song**: **“Golden”** from *KPop Demon Hunters*

### Films that dominated
Two films dominated the night’s storyline:

- **One Battle After Another** won **six Oscars**, including **Best Picture**, **Best Director**, and **Adapted Screenplay** for Paul Thomas Anderson.  
- **Sinners**, after a record **16 nominations**, won **four Oscars**, including **Best Actor for Michael B. Jordan** and **Original Screenplay for Ryan Coogler**.  

Those two results create a sourcing tension: ABC’s posted winners list identifies **Sean Penn** as Best Actor, while some press coverage summarized *Sinners* as taking **Best Actor** for **Michael B. Jordan**. Because the Academy and ABC winner pages are the stronger primary-style sources surfaced here, the safer newsroom line is that **official published winners lists available via ABC/Academy materials show Sean Penn as Best Actor**, while broader press coverage agreed that *One Battle After Another* and *Sinners* were the two most dominant films of the ceremony.

- https://abc.com/news/e964adff-12f7-4a53-b828-3ec9db4be197/category/3590739

- https://www.latimes.com/entertainment-arts/awards/live/oscars-2026-live-updates-winners-list

### Oscars takeaway
The ceremony’s biggest macro-story was the split between an eventual **Best Picture sweep for *One Battle After Another*** and a strong but slightly less all-conquering night for **Ryan Coogler’s *Sinners***, which entered the night with exceptional nomination momentum.

## 5) NASA’s Artemis II

NASA’s **Artemis II** mission launched on **1 April 2026** from **Kennedy Space Center** and returned with a **splashdown on 10 April 2026** off the coast of **San Diego**, completing what NASA described as a **nearly 10-day** crewed lunar test flight. It was the **first crewed mission of the Artemis program** and the **first human voyage around the Moon in more than half a century**.  
Sources:  
- Artemis II mission page: https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii  
- NASA launch/mission coverage: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasas-artemis-ii-mission-leaves-earth-orbit-for-flight-around-moon  
- NASA return news release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- NASA Q&A: https://www.nasa.gov/missions/nasa-answers-your-most-pressing-artemis-ii-questions

### Crew
The crew consisted of:
- **Reid Wiseman**  
- **Victor Glover**  
- **Christina Koch**  
- **Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency)  

Source: https://www.nasa.gov/feature/our-artemis-crew/

### Records and milestones
NASA said Artemis II set a record by taking humans **farther from Earth than any previous crewed mission**, surpassing **Apollo 13’s** record during the lunar flyby phase. NASA also described the mission as successfully completing its primary objectives, including:
- testing Orion’s **life-support systems**,  
- having the crew **manually pilot** Orion,  
- performing maneuvers to send Orion to the Moon and adjust course,  
- conducting a **lunar flyby**, and  
- completing a **safe re-entry and recovery**.  

Sources:  
- NASA return release: https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- NASA crew news conference summary: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=hG44qIy31ec  
- NASA science gallery note on mission distance/farthest point: https://www.nasa.gov/gallery/artemis-ii-mission-science

NASA’s own public materials also emphasize the huge audience impact: the agency later said Artemis II generated **149.4 million views** across NASA-owned mission coverage platforms.  
Source: https://www.nasa.gov/general/nasas-artemis-ii-breaks-agency-streaming-record

### Outcome
The mission outcome was a **success**: Artemis II completed its lunar free-return-style test flight, brought the crew home safely, and cleared a major operational step toward later Artemis missions, including eventual lunar landing missions.  
Sources:  
- https://www.nasa.gov/news-release/nasa-welcomes-record-setting-artemis-ii-moonfarers-back-to-earth  
- https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii

- **Seattle’s 29-13 Super Bowl LX win** gave the Seahawks a second Lombardi Trophy and made **Kenneth Walker III** the game’s MVP.  
- The **Knicks’ 2026 NBA title** ended one of American sports’ longest major-city droughts: **53 years without a championship**.  
- The **98th Oscars** centered on **One Battle After Another** and **Sinners**, the year’s two defining awards films.  
- **Artemis II** succeeded as NASA’s first crewed moon mission in decades and set a **new human-distance record from Earth**.

Short direct answer: the first half of 2026 was defined by an upset-heavy World Cup, Seattle’s Super Bowl title, the Knicks’ first championship since 1973, an Oscars night led by *One Battle After Another* and *Sinners*, and NASA’s successful, record-setting Artemis II moon flight.

baseline@60.report:
# State of the stack, mid-2026
A CTO briefing on the AI + web engineering ecosystem

## Executive summary

By mid-2026, the stack has clarified into layers:

- **Model providers** are segmenting into flagship general models, cheaper mini/nano variants, and explicit reasoning models/options.
- **LangChain 1.x** has finished its architectural reset: `create_agent` is now the standard entrypoint, legacy chains/retrievers moved to `langchain-classic`, and **LangGraph** is the runtime layer underneath.  
- **Deep Agents** has emerged as a higher-level “agent harness” on top of LangChain + LangGraph, bundling planning, subagents, filesystem access, permissions, summarization, and HITL.  
- **Next.js 16** is the current stable generation, with **Turbopack now the default bundler**, Node 20.9+ required, and several long-signaled cleanup changes now enforced.

The main strategic pattern is now clear: **use LangChain for portable agent abstractions, LangGraph for runtime/orchestration, Deep Agents for batteries-included agent apps, and Next.js 16 for frontend/server integration when you want a unified React platform.**

---

## 1) OpenAI API lineup and pricing

### What appears current in mid-2026

OpenAI’s current ecosystem appears split across:

- **Flagship general-purpose GPT-5 family**
- **Cheaper GPT-5 mini / nano tiers**
- **Reasoning-oriented families and options**, including `o4-mini` and `o3`
- **Reasoning controls** such as `reasoning_effort`

OpenAI-hosted docs are the authoritative source for model naming and behavior, but the search tooling did not surface the official pricing page reliably. I was able to ground model naming and reasoning behavior in OpenAI-hosted docs, and pricing via a secondary source that explicitly mirrors OpenAI pricing and cites the OpenAI pricing page. I’m marking that distinction clearly.

### Model names and roles

Deep Agents and LangChain docs both show current OpenAI model naming examples such as `openai:gpt-5.5`, which is useful as ecosystem evidence that GPT-5.x naming is now in active use:
- Deep Agents overview example using `openai:gpt-5.5`:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- LangChain v1 example middleware code also references `gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5-nano`, and `gpt-5.4-mini`:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1

For reasoning controls, OpenAI documentation was not directly retrievable in search, but ecosystem and forum references indicate current support for `reasoning_effort` levels across GPT-5-era models, with newer GPT-5-series models supporting richer settings including `xhigh` on some later variants:
- OpenAI community discussion referencing official docs behavior for `reasoning_effort`:  
  https://community.openai.com/t/request-for-compatibility-matrix-reasoning-effort-sampling-parameters-across-gpt-5-series/1371738

### Pricing snapshot
**Caveat:** the exact prices below come from a secondary source that states it verified against OpenAI’s pricing page. I could not directly fetch the official OpenAI pricing page through search during this run, so treat these as **high-confidence but not directly fetched from OpenAI**.

Secondary mirror:
- https://pecollective.com/tools/openai-api-pricing

Reported prices:

| Model | Input / 1M tokens | Output / 1M tokens | Notes |
|---|---:|---:|---|
| `gpt-5` | $1.25 | $10.00 | Flagship general model |
| `gpt-5-mini` | $0.25 | $2.00 | Mid-tier / cost-optimized |
| `gpt-5-nano` | $0.05 | $0.40 | Lowest-cost tier |
| `o4-mini` | $1.10 | $4.40 | Reasoning-optimized small model |
| `gpt-4.1` | $2.00 | $8.00 | Still available but no longer the newest flagship |
| `gpt-4.1-mini` | $0.40 | $1.60 | Previous-gen cost tier |
| `gpt-4.1-nano` | $0.10 | $0.40 | Previous-gen ultra-cheap tier |

The same mirror also notes **cached input discounts** for some models, e.g. GPT-4.1 with a 75% cached-input discount. Because I could not fetch OpenAI’s own pricing page directly, I would verify cache pricing before budgeting.

### Reasoning options

The key practical shift is that reasoning is no longer just “pick a reasoning model”; it is increasingly **a tunable runtime behavior**:

- Some GPT-5-series models support `reasoning_effort`
- Later GPT-5-era models appear to support values like:
  - `none`
  - `low`
  - `medium`
  - `high`
  - on some newer variants, `xhigh`

Source indicating that model support varies by generation:
- https://community.openai.com/t/request-for-compatibility-matrix-reasoning-effort-sampling-parameters-across-gpt-5-series/1371738

### CTO take

Use the lineup like this:

- **`gpt-5`**: default high-quality general model when quality matters more than cost
- **`gpt-5-mini`**: the likely default for production agent workloads needing good cost/quality
- **`gpt-5-nano`**: classification, routing, extraction, guardrails, cheap copilots
- **`o4-mini` / `o3`-style reasoning**: hard math, coding, planning, verification, agent subproblems
- **`reasoning_effort`**: treat as a latency/cost knob, not a marketing feature

### Risk note

I could not directly retrieve the official OpenAI pricing page in this session. For procurement or budgeting, re-check OpenAI’s live pricing page before committing numbers.

---

## 2) LangChain 1.x ecosystem

### Current package state

PyPI shows these current releases:

- **`langchain`** latest release: Jun 22, 2026  
  https://pypi.org/project/langchain
- **`langgraph`** latest release: Jun 29, 2026  
  https://pypi.org/project/langgraph
- **`langchain-core`** latest release: Jun 18, 2026  
  https://pypi.org/project/langchain-core
- **`langchain-classic`** latest release: Jun 10, 2026  
  https://pypi.org/project/langchain-classic

The docs position **LangChain v1** as the production-ready base for building agents:
- LangChain v1 release notes:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1

### The modern API: `create_agent`

The central fact in LangChain 1.x is:

- **`langchain.agents.create_agent` is now the standard API**
- It **replaces** the older `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent`

Official source:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1

The docs state explicitly:
- "`create_agent` is the standard way to build agents in LangChain 1.0"
- it replaces `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent`
- middleware is the main customization mechanism

### What replaced legacy patterns

LangChain 1.x deliberately reduced the surface area of the core package.

What moved out:

- legacy chains and chain implementations
- retrievers from the old `langchain.retrievers`
- indexing API
- hub module
- deprecated/community exports

Those now live in:
- **`langchain-classic`**

Official source:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1#langchain-classic

So the architectural reset is:

- **Old world**: chains, retrievers, prebuilt patterns, broad namespace
- **New world**: agents + middleware + provider-agnostic message/tool/model abstractions

### New extension model: middleware

Middleware is now the key abstraction for customizing agent behavior.

LangChain docs list built-in middleware such as:

- `PIIMiddleware`
- `SummarizationMiddleware`
- `HumanInTheLoopMiddleware`

And custom middleware hooks including:

- `before_agent`
- `before_model`
- `wrap_model_call`
- `wrap_tool_call`
- `after_model`
- `after_agent`

Official source:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1#middleware

This is a major change: instead of stitching together chains or agent executors, you now modify agent behavior through middleware.

### Runtime layer: LangGraph

LangChain’s own docs are explicit:

- **LangChain** = agent framework / abstractions / integrations
- **LangGraph** = orchestration runtime

LangGraph provides:

- durable execution
- checkpointing / persistence
- streaming
- interrupts / human-in-the-loop
- time travel
- memory primitives

Sources:
- LangGraph overview:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview
- LangGraph v1 notes:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

LangGraph v1 specifically says:
- `create_react_agent` is deprecated in favor of LangChain `create_agent`
- LangGraph remains the underlying runtime

### Practical interpretation for a CTO

Use the stack by abstraction level:

- **LangChain 1.x** when you want portable agent APIs and provider-agnostic tooling
- **LangGraph** when you need explicit workflow control, durable state, custom graphs, or production-grade long-running orchestration
- **`langchain-classic`** only for migration or legacy code

### Bottom line on LangChain

LangChain 1.x is no longer a “grab bag” framework. It is now a narrower agent framework centered on `create_agent`, middleware, and standard content blocks, with LangGraph clearly underneath.

---

## 3) Deep Agents

### Current version

PyPI shows:

- **`deepagents` 0.6.12**
- released **Jun 25, 2026**

Source:
- https://pypi.org/project/deepagents

### What Deep Agents is

The docs define Deep Agents as an **agent harness** built on top of LangChain and LangGraph, with batteries included for:

- planning
- subagents
- filesystem access
- permissions
- context management
- human approval
- long-term memory

Sources:
- Overview: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- API reference: https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

### Core API

The primary constructor is:

- **`create_deep_agent(...)`**

Official docs and reference:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

The quickstart pattern is:

```python
from deepagents import create_deep_agent

agent = create_deep_agent(
    model="openai:gpt-5.5",
    tools=[...],
    system_prompt="You are a helpful assistant",
)
```

### Subagents

Deep Agents includes a built-in **`task` tool** for spawning subagents.

Core concepts visible in the docs/reference:

- `SubAgent`
- `CompiledSubAgent`
- `SubAgentMiddleware`
- `create_sub_agent`
- default `general-purpose` subagent
- async subagent support:
  - `AsyncSubAgent`
  - `AsyncSubAgentMiddleware`

Sources:
- Overview / delegation section:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- Reference:  
  https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

This is one of the main differentiators from vanilla LangChain agents: Deep Agents bakes subagent orchestration into the default harness.

### Filesystem backends

Deep Agents exposes built-in filesystem tools:

- `ls`
- `read_file`
- `write_file`
- `edit_file`
- `glob`
- `grep`
- optionally `execute`

Docs also describe pluggable backends including:

- `StateBackend` — state-backed, ephemeral
- `StoreBackend` — LangGraph store-backed, persistent
- `FilesystemBackend` — real filesystem
- `CompositeBackend` — path-routed composite
- `ContextHubBackend`
- `LocalShellBackend`
- `LangSmithSandbox`

Sources:
- Overview:  
  https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- Reference backend classes:  
  https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

This is notable operationally: Deep Agents treats filesystem access as a first-class substrate, not an add-on tool.

### Human-in-the-loop

Deep Agents supports HITL via `interrupt_on`.

Behavior:

- configure tools that require approval
- backed by LangGraph interrupts
- requires a checkpointer to pause/resume safely
- decisions can include `approve`, `edit`, `reject`, `respond`

Official docs:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/human-in-the-loop

The docs are explicit that:
- a **checkpointer is required**
- you resume with the same thread ID
- filesystem-permission interrupts are also supported
- conditional interrupts require `langchain>=1.3.3`

### Other core capabilities

The current Deep Agents stack also includes:

- `write_todos` planning
- memory via `AGENTS.md`
- skills via `SKILL.md`
- summarization middleware
- prompt caching support on some providers
- interpreters with QuickJS via `CodeInterpreterMiddleware`
- dynamic subagents
- sandbox-backed `execute`

Relevant docs:
- Overview: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- Interpreters: https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/interpreters

### CTO take

Deep Agents is best understood as:
- **LangChain `create_agent` + LangGraph runtime + a strongly opinionated default harness**

Use it when you want:
- fast time-to-agent
- delegation/subagents
- filesystem-native behavior
- long-running task support
- built-in planning/HITL/context tooling

Do **not** use it when you want minimalism or very custom orchestration; then drop to LangChain or LangGraph directly.

---

## 4) Next.js

### Current major version

The current major version is:

- **Next.js 16**
- docs currently show **Latest Version 16.2.9**

Sources:
- Upgrade guide / docs header:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- Next.js 16 announcement:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### Runtime requirements

Official requirements:

- **Minimum Node.js: 20.9**
- **Minimum TypeScript: 5.1.0**
- Supported browsers:
  - Chrome 111+
  - Edge 111+
  - Firefox 111+
  - Safari 16.4+

Source:
- Installation docs:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/installation
- Next 16 release notes:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### Build tooling: Turbopack is now default

This is the most important tooling shift:

- **Turbopack is now the default bundler**
- `next dev` uses Turbopack by default
- `next build` also uses Turbopack by default
- You can opt back to webpack with:
  - `next dev --webpack`
  - `next build --webpack`

Sources:
- Installation docs:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/installation
- Next 16 announcement:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

The upgrade guide also says:
- if you have a **custom webpack config**, `next build` will now fail unless you explicitly deal with the Turbopack transition

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

### Key breaking changes

#### 1) `middleware.ts` → `proxy.ts`
Next.js 16 introduces `proxy.ts` as the explicit network-boundary file.

- `proxy.ts` replaces `middleware.ts`
- `proxy.ts` runs on **Node.js runtime**
- `middleware.ts` remains for Edge use cases but is deprecated

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

#### 2) Async request APIs
Synchronous access has been removed for several APIs.

Now must use async access for:
- `params`
- `searchParams`
- `cookies()`
- `headers()`
- `draftMode()`

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

#### 3) `next lint` removed
- `next lint` command removed
- `next build` no longer runs lint automatically
- use ESLint CLI or Biome directly

Sources:
- Installation docs:  
  https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/installation
- Next 16 release notes:  
  https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

#### 4) PPR / caching model cleanup
- `experimental.ppr` removed
- `experimental.dynamicIO` renamed to `cacheComponents`
- caching is now centered on:
  - `cacheComponents`
  - `"use cache"`
  - new APIs like `updateTag()` / revised `revalidateTag()`

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

#### 5) Parallel routes now require explicit `default.js`
Builds can fail without them.

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

#### 6) Image behavior/security defaults changed
Notable changes include:
- `images.minimumCacheTTL` default increased
- `images.imageSizes` default changed
- `images.qualities` default changed to `[75]`
- local `next/image` src with query strings now requires `images.localPatterns`
- local IP optimization blocked by default

Source:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

### Other notable platform changes

- **Build Adapters API** introduced in alpha
- **React Compiler support** is stable
- **Turbopack filesystem caching** available in beta
- `create-next-app` now defaults to:
  - TypeScript
  - Tailwind
  - ESLint
  - App Router
  - Turbopack
  - optionally `AGENTS.md`

Sources:
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/getting-started/installation

### CTO take

Next.js 16 is a meaningful platform consolidation release:

- Turbopack is no longer optional background tech; it is the default build system.
- The framework is forcing cleanup of old APIs (`next lint`, sync request APIs, deprecated config flags).
- If you run custom webpack-heavy builds, treat upgrade planning as a real migration project.
- If you are greenfield, the default stack is now much cleaner and faster than in the 13/14 era.

---

# Strategic recommendations

## If you are standardizing an AI application stack
Use this layering:

- **Frontend / full-stack web**: Next.js 16
- **Agent abstraction layer**: LangChain 1.x
- **Runtime / orchestration / durability**: LangGraph
- **Batteries-included agent apps**: Deep Agents
- **Model default**: a GPT-5 mini-class model for cost/performance, with a reasoning model or `reasoning_effort` escalation path

## If you have an existing LangChain codebase
Priorities:

1. move new agent work to `create_agent`
2. stop building on deprecated `create_react_agent`
3. isolate legacy chains/retrievers into `langchain-classic`
4. adopt LangGraph primitives where persistence/HITL/time-travel matter

## If you have an existing Next.js 14/15 codebase
Check for:

- custom webpack config
- `middleware.ts`
- sync `params/searchParams`
- `next lint` in CI
- old PPR or `experimental.*` caching flags
- assumptions about image defaults

---

# State of the stack, mid-2026
## CTO briefing: AI + web engineering ecosystem

This briefing summarizes where four important parts of the stack stand as of **July 4, 2026**: OpenAI’s API lineup, the LangChain 1.x ecosystem, the `deepagents` library, and Next.js. All claims below are grounded in current docs, release notes, or package indexes.

---

## 1) OpenAI API: current model lineup, pricing, and reasoning controls

### Executive take
OpenAI’s API center of gravity has moved to the **GPT-5.x family**, with a clear segmentation:

- **Flagship**: `gpt-5.5`
- **Higher-precision flagship variant**: `gpt-5.5-pro`
- **Mid-tier**: `gpt-5.4`
- **Higher-precision mid-tier variant**: `gpt-5.4-pro`
- **Lower-cost / higher-volume tiers**: `gpt-5.4-mini`, `gpt-5.4-nano`

OpenAI’s model docs explicitly position **GPT-5.5** as the default starting point for complex reasoning/coding, and **GPT-5.4 mini / nano** for lower-latency or lower-cost workloads.  
Sources:  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/all  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning

### Current lineup and positioning

| Tier | Exact model name | Official positioning |
|---|---|---|
| Flagship | `gpt-5.5` | “A new class of intelligence for coding and professional work” |
| Flagship premium | `gpt-5.5-pro` | Smarter/more precise variant of GPT-5.5 |
| Mid-tier | `gpt-5.4` | More affordable model for coding and professional work |
| Mid-tier premium | `gpt-5.4-pro` | Smarter/more precise variant of GPT-5.4 |
| High-volume / efficient | `gpt-5.4-mini` | Fast, efficient model for coding, computer use, subagents |
| Cheapest GPT-5.4-class | `gpt-5.4-nano` | Optimized for simple, high-volume tasks |

Sources:  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/all  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/changelog

### Exact published token pricing
From the official models/pricing surfaces currently exposed in OpenAI docs:

| Model | Input price | Output price | Context window | Max output |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| `gpt-5.5` | **$5 / 1M input tokens** | **$30 / 1M output tokens** | 1M | 128K |
| `gpt-5.4` | **$2.50 / 1M input tokens** | **$15 / 1M output tokens** | 1M | 128K |
| `gpt-5.4-mini` | **$0.75 / 1M input tokens** | **$4.50 / 1M output tokens** | 400K | 128K |

Sources:

- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

### Important pricing caveat
I could confirm the **exact published prices** above for `gpt-5.5`, `gpt-5.4`, and `gpt-5.4-mini` in current official docs. I **could not reliably verify exact public token prices** from official docs for:

- `gpt-5.5-pro`
- `gpt-5.4-pro`
- `gpt-5.4-nano`

The official catalog confirms these models exist, but the surfaced docs I found did not expose those exact per-token figures in a directly citable way. For budgeting, treat those prices as **must-verify live in the official pricing UI/API docs before procurement sign-off**.  
Sources:  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models/all  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing

### Reasoning options
OpenAI’s current reasoning guide says reasoning-capable models use internal reasoning tokens and are best used through the **Responses API**. The main control is `reasoning.effort`.

Documented effort values across current reasoning docs include:

- `none`
- `minimal`
- `low`
- `medium`
- `high`
- `xhigh`

Support is model-dependent. OpenAI’s docs also note:
- newer GPT-5-family models support configurable reasoning effort
- reasoning works best in the **Responses API**

Sources:

- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/changelog

- **Use `gpt-5.4`** when quality still matters but budget pressure is real
- **Use `gpt-5.4-mini`** for subagents, large-scale tool use, routing, enrichment, and high-throughput workloads

## 2) LangChain 1.x ecosystem: current versions, modern APIs, and the runtime layer

### Executive take
LangChain is now firmly in its **post-1.0** era. The key architectural shift is:

- **`create_agent` is the modern standard**
- older agent patterns have been replaced by a **middleware-centric agent API**
- **LangGraph** is the runtime/orchestration layer under LangChain agents
- legacy 0.x-era functionality has been moved aside into **`langchain-classic`** or archived docs

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

### Current versions
There are two version signals worth tracking:

#### A. Official LangChain release notes baseline
The official Python changelog highlights these milestone versions:

- `langchain v1.3.0` — May 12, 2026
- `langgraph v1.2.0` — May 12, 2026
- `deepagents v0.6.0` — May 12, 2026

Source:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/changelog

#### B. Current package index versions
PyPI now shows newer patch-level package versions beyond those milestone notes:

- `langchain` latest PyPI release: **1.3.11**  
  https://pypi.org/project/langchain
- `deepagents` latest PyPI release: **0.6.12**  
  https://pypi.org/project/deepagents

I could not confirm an exact current PyPI version for `langgraph` from a clean official/package-index result in the same way, so the safest citable runtime milestone remains the official release-note line: **LangGraph v1.2.0**.  
Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/changelog  
- https://pypi.org/project/langchain  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

### The modern agent-building API
The new standard is **`create_agent`**.

LangChain’s v1 release notes explicitly say `create_agent` replaces `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent`. It is simpler at the top level, but more extensible because it is built around **middleware**.

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langgraph-v1

### What replaced the legacy patterns
The important migration story is not just “new function name.” It is a broader replacement of older agent/chains patterns.

#### Replacements called out in official docs

| Legacy / older pattern | Modern replacement |
|---|---|
| `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent` | `langchain.agents.create_agent` |
| pre-model hooks | middleware `before_model` |
| post-model hooks | middleware `after_model` |
| ad hoc tool error handling | middleware `wrap_tool_call` |
| prompted structured output | `ToolStrategy` / `ProviderStrategy` |
| older `langchain` broad namespace | streamlined `langchain`; legacy moved to `langchain-classic` |

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1

LangChain’s own philosophy/release docs also state that the old sprawl of chains/agents was intentionally replaced with a more compact 1.x architecture.  
Source:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/philosophy

### Middleware is now the control plane
Modern LangChain agents are increasingly configured through middleware rather than one-off wrappers.

Examples called out in official docs include:

- summarization middleware
- human-in-the-loop middleware
- retries
- moderation
- dynamic model selection
- tool-call wrapping

Source:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langchain/middleware/overview

### Runtime layer: LangGraph
The runtime beneath LangChain agents is **LangGraph**. LangChain’s docs describe the relationship clearly:

- **LangChain** = opinionated agent framework
- **LangGraph** = lower-level orchestration/runtime layer

LangGraph remains the place to drop down when you need:

- custom state graphs
- explicit routing/fan-out
- checkpointing
- durable execution
- typed streaming
- human interrupts
- advanced orchestration patterns

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/workflows-agents  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/use-graph-api

### Notable runtime advances in the 1.x era
From the official changelog, the runtime story now includes:

- typed `GraphOutput`
- typed streaming formats (`version="v2"` and later event streaming evolutions)
- checkpoint/state durability
- `DeltaChannel` for more efficient state/checkpoint storage
- node/run timeout controls
- better event streaming and observability

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/changelog  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/streaming  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/observability

### CTO takeaway
LangChain 1.x is no longer best understood as “a prompt-chaining library.” It is now:

1. a slimmed-down high-level agent framework (`create_agent`)
2. powered by middleware as the main extension mechanism
3. sitting on top of **LangGraph**, which is the real runtime substrate for durable agent systems

---

## 3) `deepagents`: current version and core APIs

### Executive take
`deepagents` is LangChain’s more opinionated agent harness for long-horizon, tool-using, file-working, subagent-spawning systems. It is positioned above `create_agent`, not below it.

Sources:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview  
- https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents

### Current version
Current PyPI package version:

- **`deepagents 0.6.12`** — released **June 25, 2026**

Source:  
- https://pypi.org/project/deepagents

Note: LangChain’s official changelog milestone mentions `deepagents v0.6.0` on May 12, 2026, but PyPI shows later patch releases through `0.6.12`.  
Source:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/changelog

### Core API: agent creation
Primary Python constructor:

- **`create_deep_agent(...)`**

Primary JS/TS constructor:

- **`createDeepAgent(...)`**

Sources:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents/graph/create_deep_agent  
- https://reference.langchain.com/javascript/deepagents

The docs position it as a pre-wired execution graph with:

- tool loop
- planning/todo support
- filesystem tools
- context management
- subagents
- skills/memory
- optional HITL controls

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

### Built-in tool surface
The docs show that deep agents come with a default tool environment including:

- `write_todos`
- `ls`
- `read_file`
- `write_file`
- `edit_file`
- `glob`
- `grep`
- `task` for subagent delegation

Sources:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents/graph/create_deep_agent  
- https://reference.langchain.com/javascript/deepagents

### Subagents
Subagent delegation is a core feature, not an add-on. The official docs describe deep agents as supporting:

- subagent spawning
- isolated context windows
- delegation via a `task` tool
- composition with custom LangGraph subgraphs

A key extensibility point: any LangGraph `CompiledStateGraph` can be used as a subagent.

Sources:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview

### Filesystem backends
The backend model is one of the most important parts of `deepagents`.

Core backend classes documented in the Python reference:

- `StateBackend`
- `StoreBackend`
- `FilesystemBackend`
- `CompositeBackend`
- `BaseSandbox`

Sources:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents/backends  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/backends

#### Backend roles

| Backend | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `StateBackend` | Ephemeral files stored in agent state |
| `StoreBackend` | Persistent files in LangGraph `BaseStore` |
| `FilesystemBackend` | Direct host filesystem access |
| `CompositeBackend` | Route different paths to different backends |
| `BaseSandbox` | Sandbox abstraction for execution environments |

Source:  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents/backends

This is a strong design choice for enterprise use, because it separates:

- scratch state
- durable memory/artifacts
- host-level filesystem access
- sandboxed execution

### Human-in-the-loop
The docs describe human control as a first-class part of the harness through:

- approval workflows
- interrupts / steering
- human-in-the-loop middleware concepts

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

### CTO takeaway
`deepagents` is effectively LangChain’s “opinionated production harness” for:
- multi-step agent work
- file-centric workflows
- subagent decomposition
- persistent/durable state
- human checkpoints

If `create_agent` is the lightweight default, `deepagents` is the bundled higher-level harness for agentic applications that need working memory, planning, and delegation.

---

## 4) Next.js: current major version, runtime requirements, breaking changes, build tooling

### Executive take
The current major line is **Next.js 16**. In mid-2026, the safest stable target is the **16.2.x** line, while **16.3** is present as preview/recent release activity depending on channel. The official upgrade docs currently show **“Latest Version 16.2.10”**.

The most important migration-impact changes called out by official docs:

- major Turbopack fixes/improvements
- faster startup/rendering
- hydration/debugging improvements
- more agent/AI-oriented tooling

Source:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

### Security note
The Next.js blog includes important security advisories in the 15.x/16.x era, including a critical React Server Components RCE advisory and other issues affecting broad version ranges. This makes staying current on 16.x patches operationally important, not optional.

Source:  
- https://nextjs.org/blog

### CTO takeaway
By mid-2026, Next.js is no longer just “the React framework.” It is now also:
- a compiler/build-platform decision
- a runtime-baseline decision (Node 20.9+)
- a caching/orchestration model decision
- increasingly an AI-tooling-aware framework

Turbopack is the strategic path; webpack is the escape hatch.

---

# Recommendations

## If you are standardizing an AI app stack in mid-2026
### Model layer
- Default to **OpenAI Responses API**
- Use:
  - `gpt-5.5` for premium reasoning/coding
  - `gpt-5.4` for balanced cost/quality
  - `gpt-5.4-mini` for high-volume agent/subagent workloads

Sources:  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/reasoning  
- https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/models

### Agent framework layer
- Use **LangChain 1.x**
- Start with **`create_agent`**
- Drop to **LangGraph** for custom orchestration
- Use **`deepagents`** when the application needs planning, files, subagents, and stronger operational scaffolding

Sources:  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1  
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/deepagents

### Web/frontend layer
- Standardize new work on **Next.js 16.2.x**
- Move platform baselines to:
  - Node 20.9+
  - TS 5.1+
- Treat **Turbopack** as default and webpack as fallback-only

Sources:  
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-2

---

# Bottom line
Mid-2026 looks like this: **OpenAI’s GPT-5.x family is the API center of gravity; LangChain 1.x has consolidated around `create_agent` on top of LangGraph; `deepagents` has matured into an opinionated file/subagent/human-loop harness; and Next.js 16, with Node 20.9+ and Turbopack by default, is the current frontend/platform baseline.**

It is grounded in current documentation and release sources. Where an exact detail was not surfaced cleanly from first-party docs, I say so explicitly.

# 2) LangChain 1.x ecosystem

## Current state

LangChain’s ecosystem has materially simplified in 1.x:

- **LangChain 1.x** = high-level agent framework
- **LangGraph 1.x** = runtime/orchestration layer
- **langchain-classic** = compatibility bucket for older abstractions

Sources:  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1

## Versions

The official docs clearly establish that the current architecture is the **1.x line** for both LangChain and LangGraph.  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

I also confirmed a current PyPI release date for `langchain` of **June 22, 2026**, but the quick-search result did not expose the exact version number inline. So the safe statement is:

- **LangChain:** current stable line is **1.x**
- **LangGraph:** current stable line is **1.x**
- verify exact patch version on PyPI at upgrade time

PyPI / docs:
- https://pypi.org/project/langchain
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

## The modern agent-building API

The current standard entry point is:

- **`langchain.agents.create_agent`**

Official release and migration docs explicitly say `create_agent` is now the standard way to build agents.  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langchain-v1  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1

This is the key design shift: the ecosystem now has a much clearer “golden path” for new work.

## What replaced the legacy patterns

### Old default
Before v1, LangChain commonly pushed people toward:
- chain-based patterns
- multiple overlapping agent constructors
- `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent`

### New default
Now:
- **`create_agent`** is the recommended default
- legacy chain/retriever/indexing patterns moved into **`langchain-classic`**
- `langgraph.prebuilt.create_react_agent` is no longer the recommended primary entry point

Sources:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

### Specific migration examples

Moved or relegated to legacy/compatibility usage:
- `LLMChain`
- `ConversationChain`
- older retriever imports
- older indexing APIs
- some old `hub`/embedding patterns

Source:  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/migrate/langchain-v1

## Runtime layer: LangGraph

LangGraph is now the explicit runtime substrate for:
- stateful agent execution
- checkpointing
- persistence
- streaming
- human-in-the-loop
- durable workflows

Docs:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview
- https://reference.langchain.com/python/langgraph
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/releases/langgraph-v1

The cleanest mental model is:

| Layer | Role |
|---|---|
| **LangChain** | Developer-facing framework for building agents quickly |
| **LangGraph** | Runtime/orchestration engine for durable, stateful execution |
| **LangSmith** | Tracing, evals, observability, and ops |

LangGraph overview:  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/langgraph/overview

## CTO take

LangChain is less of a “grab bag” than it was in 0.x. In mid-2026, the recommendation is straightforward:

- Build new agents with **LangChain 1.x `create_agent`**
- Drop to **LangGraph** only when you need runtime control
- Fence old chain-heavy code inside **`langchain-classic`** and stop expanding it

# 3) deepagents

## Current version status

The `deepagents` story is a little messy because the project is moving quickly.

### What I could confirm

- PyPI shows `deepagents` as a current package with a latest release on **June 25, 2026**  
  https://pypi.org/project/deepagents
- GitHub releases show **`deepagents==0.7.0a3`** on **July 1, 2026**, explicitly as a **pre-release**  
  https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents/releases

So the accurate summary is:

- **Current stable/public package exists on PyPI**
- **Latest observed core code tag is pre-release `0.7.0a3`**
- `deepagents` is still **pre-1.0** and should be treated accordingly

## What deepagents is

Official docs describe Deep Agents as an **agent harness** built on LangGraph, with built-in support for:
- planning
- subagents
- filesystems
- context management
- steering / HITL

Overview:
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview

## Core APIs and capabilities

## Agent execution

The quickstart shows a standard invocation model:

- install with `pip install deepagents`
- run the agent with **`agent.invoke({...})`**
- pass a chat-style `messages` array

Quickstart:  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/quickstart

Example shape from docs:

```python
result = agent.invoke({
    "messages": [{
        "role": "user",
        "content": "What is langgraph?"
    }]
})
```

## Agent construction / customization

The public release notes show construction customization such as:

- **`create_deep_agent(..., middleware=[...])`**

GitHub release notes mention middleware replacement behavior, including `SummarizationMiddleware`.  
https://github.com/langchain-ai/deepagents/releases

## Subagents

Subagents are a first-class part of the design, not a bolt-on. Docs and release notes describe:

- isolated context windows
- built-in delegation
- inline subagents
- async/remote subagents

Sources:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- https://www.langchain.com/blog/deep-agents-v0-5

### Inline vs async subagents

The v0.5 announcement explains two modes:

- **inline subagents**: supervisor waits
- **async subagents**: returns task ID, work continues remotely

Source:  
https://www.langchain.com/blog/deep-agents-v0-5

## Filesystem backends and file model

Deepagents has a distinctive **filesystem-centric** architecture. Official overview breaks the environment into layers including:

- tools
- virtual filesystem
- filesystem permissions
- code execution

Overview:  
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview

The docs explicitly say the virtual filesystem is backed by **pluggable backends**, but the surfaced docs here do **not** give a clean backend matrix with exact class names for every backend. So the safe statement is:

- deepagents supports **pluggable filesystem backends**
- file tools like `write_file` and `read_file` are built in
- exact backend implementation choices should be verified in source/reference before standardizing across the org

Sources:
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview
- https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/quickstart

## Human-in-the-loop

HITL is a core feature, not an add-on. Official docs mention:

- human-in-the-loop approval
- interrupts
- steering

Overview:
https://docs.langchain.com/oss/python/deepagents/overview

And the managed runtime docs extend this with:
- durable runs
- thread state
- sandboxes
- traces
- revisions

Managed Deep Agents:
https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/managed-deep-agents-overview

## Managed/runtime status

LangChain also offers **Managed Deep Agents**, but the docs say it is in **private beta** and limited to **LangSmith Cloud in the US region**.  
https://docs.langchain.com/langsmith/managed-deep-agents-overview

## CTO take

Deepagents is compelling if you want an opinionated harness for:
- multi-step agents
- subagent decomposition
- file-backed context management
- HITL controls

But it is still a **fast-moving pre-1.0 project**. Treat it as:
- promising
- powerful
- not yet as lifecycle-stable as LangChain/LangGraph 1.x

# 4) Next.js

## Current major version

The current major version is **Next.js 16**.  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

The official docs show **16.2.10** as the current stable docs baseline, while **16.3** is still preview.  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16-3-instant-navigations

## Runtime requirements

Next.js 16 requires:

- **Node.js 20.9.0+**
- **TypeScript 5.1.0+**
- supported browsers:
  - Chrome 111+
  - Edge 111+
  - Firefox 111+
  - Safari 16.4+

Source:  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

This is a meaningful floor bump. If you still have Node 18 in CI, containers, or internal tooling, you are behind.

## Key breaking changes

## 1. Turbopack is now the default bundler

Starting with Next.js 16:
- `next dev` uses Turbopack by default
- `next build` uses Turbopack by default

Sources:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

This is the biggest practical shift in build tooling.

## 2. `next lint` is removed

The old framework-level lint command is gone. `next build` also no longer runs linting.

You now need to run linting explicitly through:
- ESLint CLI
- Biome
- or your own CI command

Source:  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

## 3. Runtime config is removed

Removed:
- `serverRuntimeConfig`
- `publicRuntimeConfig`

Use env vars and modern server/client boundaries instead.  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

## 4. `middleware.ts` is deprecated in favor of `proxy.ts`

This is a naming and convention shift the docs call out.  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

## 5. Parallel routes are stricter

Next.js 16 requires explicit `default.js` files for parallel route slots; builds fail otherwise.  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16

## 6. Old config names are removed

- `experimental.turbo` → replaced by **`turbopack`**
- older `devIndicators` options removed
- AMP support removed

Sources:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/config/next-config-js/turbopack
- https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

## Current build tooling reality

## Turbopack is the strategic default

Turbopack is now:
- stable
- default for dev
- default for build

Docs:
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack  
https://nextjs.org/blog/next-16

## But Turbopack is not a perfect webpack clone

Important caveats in official docs:

- webpack plugins are not supported by Turbopack
- `webpack()` config is not honored by Turbopack
- some Sass customizations still require webpack
- some platforms without native bindings fall back to WASM and lose Turbopack support

Source:  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack

### Native runtime support matters

Turbopack native bindings exist for mainstream:
- macOS
- Windows
- Linux
- x64 / ARM64
- glibc / musl variants

On platforms like FreeBSD/OpenBSD, docs indicate fallback behavior without Turbopack support.  
https://nextjs.org/docs/app/api-reference/turbopack

## Point release status

As of early July 2026:
- **16.2.x** is the safe stable family
- **16.3** is preview, not the default enterprise target yet

Sources:
- https://nextjs.org/docs/app/guides/upgrading/version-16
- https://nextjs.org/blog

## CTO take

For production estates, the practical posture is:

- standardize on **Next.js 16.2.x**
- move infra to **Node 20.9+**
- assume **Turbopack-first**, not webpack-first
- audit all custom webpack hooks and lint/build assumptions before upgrading
